segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Stuart Wilson, a man in a black tuxedo, holding an Oscar statuette, before transitioning to a wide shot of him and Mark Taylor, both in tuxedos, on stage against a deep blue, starry backdrop with swirling light patterns. Stuart Wilson, wearing glasses and a bow tie, speaks into the microphone with a British accent, thanking the entire '1917' sound team for their 'outstanding work,' including 'my guys in the trenches' and 'Mark's team in the studio,' as a lower-third graphic identifies them as 'SOUND MIXING MARK TAYLOR, STUART WILSON "1917"'. He continues, expressing gratitude for 'incredible support,' mentioning families like Victoria and Ava, producers Callum, Jane, and Pippa, and finally Sam Mendes, stating, 'None of us would be here without you. Thank you.' Loud applause and cheering erupt from the audience, with the camera cutting to show Tom Hanks clapping warmly, followed by Al Pacino and others applauding. The scene returns to the stage where Mark Taylor, also in a tuxedo with glasses, takes the microphone, holding the Oscar, and speaks with a British accent, thanking 'Ollie and his great team,' Rachel, Mike Fenton, Jambo, Lee Smith (their editor), 'all the guys who gave us such a great score to work with,' Robbie and the guys at Goldcrest, and Rob Carson, as applause briefly swells again. The camera then pulls back to a wide shot of the two winners standing with a woman in a white gown, likely a presenter, before cutting to a new pair of presenters: a woman in a navy blue gown with a diamond necklace and a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, holding a card. The woman in the navy blue gown speaks with a clear, authoritative tone, mentioning 'Jodie and Louis drivers' and having 'made multiple films' with 'an enormous amount of experience on film sets,' emphasizing understanding 'the importance' and 'primacy.' Throughout the segment, the ABC logo is visible in the bottom right corner, and on-screen promotional text overlays like 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO', 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE', and a QR code with 'Buy code' appear intermittently, indicating promotional content. The atmosphere remains formal and celebratory, with the stage lighting emphasizing the blue, cosmic backdrop and the golden Oscar statuette.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a black screen featuring a QR code labeled 'Buy code', quickly transitioning to a formal awards ceremony stage. A man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie and a woman in a navy blue spaghetti-strap gown adorned with a large diamond necklace stand at a podium. The woman, speaking with a dramatic, performative tone, begins to present, humorously detailing the non-filming duties of a 'cinematographer,' such as preparing meals and knocking on trailer doors, which elicits chuckles and applause from the audience. Intermittently, on-screen promotional text like 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' and 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE' appears, along with another QR code. The man then takes over, continuing the comedic description of the 'cinematographer' waiting in a golf cart, also drawing audience laughter and applause. The camera briefly cuts to the audience, showing a man in a tuxedo smiling and pointing towards the stage, accompanied by a woman in a sparkly gown. Returning to the presenters, the woman concludes her part, honoring these 'unsung cinematic heroes,' again met with audience appreciation. However, the man, touching his ear as if adjusting an earpiece, interjects with a confused 'Wait. What?' and states he's 'being told that...' The woman, looking at him with concern, prompts 'What?', to which he questions if 'That is not the role of the cinematographer?' The woman responds with a surprised 'Oh,' as the man adds that it 'has something to do with camera.' The woman, now clearly confused, asks 'Who who is talking to you?', and the man, looking flustered, replies, 'I I have no idea,' as the audience's laughter and applause continue, highlighting the unexpected turn in the presentation. The ABC logo remains visible in the bottom right corner throughout the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a man in a black tuxedo adjusting his earpiece, followed by a woman in a navy blue spaghetti-strap gown with a large diamond necklace standing beside him at a microphone on a grand, futuristic stage displaying 'CINEMATOGRAPHY'. The woman playfully asks, "Do you not have an earpiece?" and "Hello?", then comments, "No, I didn't, I don't think anything is in there," eliciting laughter and applause, to which the man responds, "Oh, okay." She then formally announces, "Here are the nominees for achievement in cinematography," as the camera pulls back to reveal the full stage and audience. An orchestral score begins as a montage of film clips from nominated movies plays on screen, with the man in the tuxedo announcing each: "The Irishman, Rodrigo Prieto," "Joker, Lawrence Sher," "The Lighthouse, Jarin Blaschke," "1917, Roger Deakins," and "Once Upon a Time in Hollywood, Robert Richardson." The clips showcase diverse visual styles—from gritty urban chaos to desolate lighthouses and sweeping war landscapes—each accompanied by on-screen text identifying the film and cinematographer. The presenters are shown again, with the man building suspense, "And the Oscar goes to..." before declaring, "Roger Deakins!" to a burst of applause and celebratory music. The woman adds, "This is his second Oscar." The camera cuts to the audience, where Tom Hanks and Al Pacino are seen clapping warmly, with Tom Hanks smiling broadly and Al Pacino looking pleased, all while the ABC logo remains visible in the bottom right corner, emphasizing the formal and celebratory atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a warmly smiling, bearded man in a dimly lit audience, quickly cutting to a grand awards ceremony stage. Loud, sustained applause and cheers erupt as a man with white hair and glasses, identified by a voice exclaiming "Roger Deakins!", embraces another man in a black suit. The formally dressed audience continues to applaud enthusiastically. The scene then transitions to a cinematic clip from the film '1917', showing soldiers in World War I uniforms moving through a trench, accompanied by a subtle, dramatic film score and on-screen text crediting Roger Deakins as the cinematographer. Promotional overlays like a QR code with 'Buy code' and 'WATCHING' appear intermittently. The video returns to the stage, where Roger Deakins, still amidst strong applause, walks toward the podium with a woman in a navy blue gown who hands him the Oscar statuette. Standing at the microphone, visibly emotional, he adjusts his glasses and touches his face, beginning his acceptance speech with a calm, slightly raspy voice, saying "Thank you all." He continues, thanking his "fellow nominees for their wonderfully inspiring work" and their "friendship." A humorous aside, "I'm pretty good cook actually," elicits chuckles from the audience. He dedicates the award, stating it "belongs to a lot of people that worked on this movie," specifically mentioning his wife James and his assistant and focus puller, Andy Harris, who is "somewhere out there." This mention sparks renewed applause and cheers. Deakins praises Harris as the "pinball wizard of focus pulling," adding with a touch of humor that "you'll never know what he does... you only know he makes a mistake and he never does," drawing more appreciative laughter and applause before his speech is cut off as he mentions "Peter Cavacuti." Throughout, the ABC logo is visible, and further promotional text like 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' and 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE' appear.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an upbeat, celebratory musical score accompanying a dynamic, futuristic stage design. A large, curved screen displays a montage of behind-the-scenes cinematography footage from various films. A female voice announces, "We go creative," then proceeds to name the films and their cinematographers: "Joker, Lawrence Sher," "The Lighthouse, Jarin Blaschke," "1917, Roger Deakins," and "Once Upon a Time in Hollywood, Robert Richardson." The visuals transition between film sets, camera rigs, and dramatic scenes, emphasizing the artistry of cinematography, all while the stage is illuminated with glowing white lines and a starry backdrop. The scene then cuts to the live awards ceremony, where a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie and a woman in a navy blue spaghetti-strap gown with a large diamond necklace stand at a podium. The male presenter's voice announces with anticipation, "And the Oscar goes to..." followed by a dramatic pause before he declares, "Roger Deakins!" This announcement is met with a burst of loud applause and cheering from the audience, including notable figures like Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt, who are seen clapping warmly. The celebratory music swells as the female presenter's voice adds, "Nomination for Roger Deakins!" Roger Deakins, the winner, is then seen walking to the stage, embracing the presenter, and taking the stage to sustained applause and music. He holds the Oscar statuette and begins an emotional acceptance speech, adjusting his glasses and touching his face, his voice heard saying, "Thank you. Wow. Um, I want to thank my fellow... I want to thank my fellow nominees for their wonderfully inspiring work." The stage backdrop features a large, illuminated circular sign reading 'CINEMATOGRAPHY' against a dark, starry background with golden light streaks. Throughout the segment, on-screen promotional text like 'Buy code' with a QR code, 'WATCHING,' 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO,' and 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE' appears intermittently, while the ABC logo is consistently displayed in the bottom right corner.

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens with a close-up of Roger Deakins, a man with white, wavy hair, wearing a black suit, white shirt, and black tie, standing at a podium on a grand stage, holding an Oscar statuette. He appears emotional, frequently touching his face and adjusting his hair as he delivers his acceptance speech, humorously stating, "I'm a pretty good cook actually," before dedicating the award to the many people who worked on the movie. The stage features a sleek, modern design with diagonal light streaks and a dark, starry backdrop, with the ABC logo visible in the bottom right. Deakins thanks his wife, James, who worked on it with him, prompting applause from the vast, dimly lit auditorium. He then thanks his assistant and focus puller, Andy Harris, describing him as a "wizard of focus pulling" who "you'll never know what he does" because "he only notices it if he makes a mistake and he never does," which elicits laughter and more applause from the audience. The camera cuts to the audience, showing a woman in a brown sleeveless top and another woman in a black dress clapping warmly. Deakins continues, thanking Peter Cavacuti and Charlie Rizik, calling them "two fantastic operators," along with Gary Hims and his grip crew, and John Higgins, his gaffer and a friend for "about 30 odd years." A wide shot reveals the auditorium filled with attendees, their lights twinkling, as the CINEMATOGRAPHY sign is visible above the stage. The camera alternates between close-ups of Deakins’ expressive face and wider views, capturing the celebratory atmosphere as he concludes by thanking them and their "wonderful team," expressing that they would all want him to say "thank you." The segment concludes with Deakins waving to the audience, still holding the Oscar, as the crowd applauds.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Roger Deakins, a man with white, wavy hair, wearing a black suit, white shirt, and black tie, holding an Oscar statuette as he delivers an emotional acceptance speech on a grand stage with a dark, starry backdrop and golden light streaks. He expresses his gratitude, stating it's the "most wonderful experience" and that he'll "never forget it," as the audience erupts in applause and cheering. The camera then cuts to a wide shot of the large, formally dressed crowd clapping and cheering, with visible figures including the Woman in Brown Sleeveless Top and the Woman in Black Dress, both smiling warmly. The scene transitions to a new segment where a Woman in a Navy Blue Spaghetti-Strap Gown with a large diamond necklace and a Man in a Black Tuxedo with a Bow Tie stand at a podium, presenting an award. The Woman in Navy Blue Gown speaks into the microphone with a poised and supportive demeanor, announcing, "Okay, so, um, so that was the cinematography. Yes. Our next category is film editing." The Man in Tuxedo, reading from a card, humorously interjects, "Oh, we know all about film editors. They're the ones who like to cut out a lot of my lines." The Woman in Navy Blue Gown playfully agrees, "Oh yeah. Yeah. They did a real number on me this year. Did you know that I was originally in 1917?" The Man in Tuxedo responds with surprise and appreciation, "No. Yeah. Yeah. And Parasite. Oh my god, what a wonderful year." She continues, "I know. Well, listen to this. It was originally Ford versus Ferrari versus Farrow." The Man in Tuxedo exclaims, "What? Yes. What?" and then humorously declares, "And I'm I'm still not a lot of the key art. Oh my god. Unbelievable. I'm just gonna say it, I hate this award." The Woman in Navy Blue Gown concurs, "Oh yeah, it's awful. It's an awful award. Oh, hello? Hello?" as the audience laughs and applauds. The camera briefly cuts to the audience, showing a woman in a black sequined gown clapping warmly, and then returns to the presenters. The lighting remains bright and celebratory, with the stage backdrop featuring a dark, starry design with golden accents. The ABC logo is visible in the bottom right corner throughout the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a close-up of a man in a tuxedo, as background chatter anticipates an announcement, with a female voice noting, "He's about to say the nominations." The scene transitions to a wide shot of a grand awards stage where a woman in a navy blue spaghetti-strap gown and a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie stand at a podium. The man, the presenter, gestures as he formally announces, "Here are the nominees for achievement in film editing," followed by a female voice confirming, "Yeah, he just did it." The camera then cuts to a dynamic, futuristic montage of curved screens displaying various film clips and titles, including 'Ford v Ferrari,' 'The Irishman,' 'Jojo Rabbit,' 'Joker,' and 'Parasite,' each accompanied by on-screen text identifying the film and key contributors, as the male presenter reads out each film and its associated editor(s). This montage, set against a dark, starry backdrop with golden light streaks, creates a celebratory and high-tech atmosphere, punctuated by audience applause after each nomination. The scene returns to the stage, where the woman in the navy blue gown reads from her card, announcing, "And the Oscar goes to... Michael McCusker and Andrew Buckland!" to enthusiastic applause and cheering. She continues, providing details: "This is the first Oscar and nomination for Andrew Buckland. This is also the first win and second nomination for Michael McCusker." The camera then follows a bald man in a tuxedo walking through the audience, who is later seen on stage receiving an award amidst continued applause and celebratory music. The video concludes with a clip from 'Ford v Ferrari,' showing a man in a white shirt and dark jacket standing beside a vintage car on a runway at night, with the film's title and the names 'Michael McCusker and Andrew Buckland' displayed on screen, visually confirming the winners. Throughout the segment, promotional text overlays like 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' and a QR code appear, while the ABC logo is consistently visible in the bottom right corner.

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens with a black screen displaying a 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' prompt before transitioning to a brightly lit awards ceremony stage. Two men, Michael McCusker and Andrew Buckland, both in black tuxedos with bow ties and glasses, stand at a podium, each holding an Oscar statuette for their Film Editing win for 'Ford v Ferrari'. McCusker, with a beard, holds a card and begins to speak, thanking the Academy, fellow nominees for their "incredible work," and acknowledging their "stellar picture crew" and "outstanding sound team." He then dedicates the award to his "amazing and beautiful wife, Maria," and "our son Lucas," expressing "I love you so much, Te quiero mucho," before extending thanks to his sister Claire, Joe, Armando, and his mom and dad "who supported me from the very beginning," concluding with "I love you so much" and a "un beso grande" to his family in Argentina, as applause and cheering erupt from the audience. The camera shows a woman in a blue dress and another in a black dress, both smiling and clapping, while the ABC logo is visible in the bottom right. Buckland, bald, then takes the microphone, praising "James Mangold" as "one of the best directors in this business in the last 15 years," and thanking the "great ensemble cast" led by Matt and Christian, their DP Phedon Papamichael, producers Jenno Topping and Peter Chernin, and "everyone at 20th Century." Throughout their speeches, on-screen text overlays like 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE' and a QR code for 'Buy code' appear intermittently. Buckland raises his arm in celebration, and a man in the audience with dark hair and glasses is briefly shown clapping enthusiastically before the camera returns to the stage as the men conclude their remarks amidst the formal, prestigious setting.

Both wear glasses and bow ties; McCusker has a beard and holds a card, while Buckland is bald. They speak at the podium, with McCusker gesturing and Buckland listening attentively. The camera cuts to the audience, showing reactions including a woman in a blue dress and another in a black dress, both smiling and clapping. The men continue their acceptance speech, with Buckland raising his arm in celebration. The atmosphere is celebratory, with bright stage lighting and a formal, prestigious setting.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dark, blurred transition before revealing two men in black tuxedos on stage, each holding a golden Oscar statuette, celebrating their win. The man with dark hair, a beard, and glasses, wearing a bow tie, speaks, thanking the “tireless Aaron Downey, Teca Liano, Steve Asbell, and the inimitable Emma Watts,” before expressing love for his daughter “Lena” and “Bob,” all while enthusiastic applause and uplifting orchestral music fill the air. The camera then cuts to the audience, where two women, one with blonde hair in a bun and a black dress, and another with dark hair in a blue off-the-shoulder gown, clap enthusiastically. The scene shifts back to the stage, showing the two winners walking off as a new man, introduced by a female voice as “casting director and Academy President, David Rubin,” ascends the stage steps. David Rubin, in a black tuxedo with glasses and a bow tie, stands at the podium against a backdrop of swirling, metallic silver patterns, speaking with expressive hand gestures about the event being the highlight of the Academy's year, a coming milestone, and the history of the Academy's museum, founded under Douglas Fairbanks, leading to a “special announcement.” The ABC logo is visible in the bottom right corner throughout, underscoring the formal and celebratory atmosphere with bright stage lighting and a grand, modern set.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a dark, blurred transition before revealing two men in black tuxedos on stage, each holding a golden Oscar statuette. The man on the left is bald with glasses and a bow tie, while the man on the right has dark hair, a beard, glasses, and also wears a bow tie; both appear to be celebrating a win. The camera then cuts to the audience, where two women are seen clapping enthusiastically—one with blonde hair in a bun wearing a black dress, and the other with dark hair in a blue off-the-shoulder gown. The scene shifts back to the stage, showing the two winners walking off, followed by a new man in a black tuxedo with glasses and a bow tie ascending the stage steps. He stands at the podium, speaking with expressive hand gestures and a composed demeanor, against a backdrop of swirling, metallic silver patterns. The ABC logo is visible in the bottom right corner throughout the segment, indicating the broadcast network. The atmosphere is celebratory and formal, with bright stage lighting and a grand, modern set design featuring curved, illuminated architectural elements.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a man, identified as David, wearing round, dark-rimmed glasses and a black tuxedo with a bow tie, standing on a grand, futuristic stage with illuminated white lines and a dynamic, swirling silver and blue background. An upbeat, orchestral score plays as David speaks with a composed and expressive demeanor, gesturing as he describes a “state-of-the-art showcase of moviemaking.” He then introduces “two-time Oscar winner, Tom Hanks,” prompting enthusiastic audience applause and cheers. Tom Hanks, also in a black tuxedo, walks onto the stage, shakes David’s hand, and takes the microphone. He thanks David and delivers a humorous speech, punctuated by audience laughter and applause, about the diverse museums in the “City of Angels,” including one dedicated to selfies, before emphasizing the significance of a new museum for motion pictures, humorously adding that he was “putting up some drywall” there. Throughout the segment, the camera alternates between close-ups of the speakers and wider stage shots, while promotional text like 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE' and 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' appears in the top left, and the ABC logo remains in the bottom right corner, all under bright, focused lighting.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie and glasses, identified as the Host, speaking on a stage with a dynamic, abstract background of swirling, metallic patterns. He humorously mentions "Scarlett Johansson" and "Brad Pitt was on the roof working with his shirt off," followed by "As was Colin Jost," eliciting audience laughter and applause. He continues, stating, "And dude, that was no contest." The ABC logo is visible in the bottom right, with promotional text 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' in the top left. The camera alternates between close-ups of the Host and wider shots, then cuts to the audience, where a woman in a red sequined gown and another in a brown sleeveless top are seen clapping, contributing to the audible applause. The Host then proudly announces, "We're all very proud of what has been accomplished so far in the landmark that is taking shape at Fairfax and Wilshire... And it is a pleasure to announce that the Academy Museum of Motion Pictures will open its doors on December 14th of 2020," which is met with significant applause and the start of background music. The scene transitions to a comedic stage performance featuring two individuals in full-body animal costumes: one in a black-and-white cat suit with a top hat and the other in an orange-and-white fox suit, both dancing and gesturing humorously in front of a microphone on a circular stage with warm, glowing lights in the background, accompanied by the continuing music and audience applause. The Host's voice briefly returns, mentioning "As cos members of the Motion Picture Academy," as the performance continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with dynamic, blurred warm lights, transitioning to a close-up of a woman in a shimmering silver gown laughing joyfully in the audience, as a female announcer's voice declares with clear, professional diction, "Nobody more than us understands the importance of good visual effects." This is followed by a burst of applause and cheering from the audience, as the scene quickly shifts to a comedic stage performance featuring a man in a black cat costume and a woman in an orange fox costume dancing playfully with exaggerated movements against a backdrop of golden, glowing lights. The camera then cuts to a wide, sweeping aerial view of the grand, circular awards stage and the vast, seated audience, before focusing on a woman in a sparkling orange sequined gown, smiling warmly and clapping amidst the continued applause. The announcer then states, "Here are the nominees for achievement in visual effects," accompanied by more applause, as the segment transitions into a high-energy montage of behind-the-scenes and cinematic clips from nominated films. The first film, 'Avengers: Endgame,' is introduced by the announcer naming its visual effects team – "Dan DeLeeuw, Russell Earl, Matt Aitken, and Dan Sudick" – while showing a man in a motion-capture suit, another in a blue robe, and a green-screen action sequence, with the team's names also appearing on-screen. Next, 'The Irishman' is presented with its team – "Pablo Helman, Leandro Estebecorena, Nelson Sepulveda-Fauser, and Stephane Grabli" – alongside a split-screen clip of a man in a red fez, with the names displayed on-screen. 'The Lion King' follows, with the announcer listing "Robert Legato, Adam Valdez, Andrew R. Jones, and Elliot Newman" as its visual effects team, as a black-and-white animated scene featuring giraffes, a zebra, and a warthog plays, also showing the names on-screen. Then, '1917' is introduced with "Guillaume Rocheron, Greg Butler, and Dominic Tuohy" as its team, showing a dramatic, silhouetted figure in a war-torn landscape with a cross and burning buildings, with the names on-screen. The montage concludes with 'Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker,' its team – "Roger Guyett, Neal Scanlan, Patrick Tubach, and Dominic Tuohy" – announced as a close-up of a decaying, gray face with smoke and debris is shown, with the names on-screen. Throughout this celebratory sequence, which highlights the technical artistry of the nominees, the ABC logo remains in the bottom right corner, and the segment ends with continued applause.

Throughout, the ABC logo remains in the bottom right corner, and on-screen text identifies each film and its visual effects team, creating a celebratory, high-energy sequence that highlights the technical artistry of the nominees.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a whimsical, upbeat musical score, featuring xylophone-like sounds and what resembles light applause, accompanying a comedic performance on a grand stage. A man in a black cat costume with a top hat and white face paint, and a woman in an orange-and-white fox costume with fluffy ears and tail, perform a playful, exaggerated dance under warm golden lights. The scene briefly shifts to an audience, showing a man in a black tuxedo shaking hands with another, while a woman in a teal gown smiles. This transitions into a dramatic cinematic montage from the film '1917', depicting soldiers in a war-torn battlefield under a gray sky with biplanes overhead, all while the whimsical music continues. The scene then returns to the awards stage, where a female announcer's voice declares, "And the Oscar goes to... 1917!" This is met with loud applause and cheering. The announcer continues, identifying the winners: "This is the second Oscar and second nomination for Guillaume Rocheron, and first win and second nomination for Greg Butler, and the first win and third nomination for Dominic Tuohy." Three men in black tuxedos—Guillaume Rocheron, Greg Butler, and Dominic Tuohy—walk onto the stage, holding Oscar statuettes, as the on-screen text confirms their win for 'Visual Effects' for '1917'. They stand together at the podium against a golden, circular backdrop with sparkling lights. The whimsical music continues as Guillaume Rocheron steps forward to speak, expressing gratitude: "Well, thank you for the Academy for this tremendous honor." He describes '1917' as "what you call a dream project," an "opportunity to challenge how we approach visual effects," and a "chance to collaborate with some truly fantastic people," before beginning to thank "Sam Mendes." The ABC logo and promotional overlays ('PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO', 'Buy code' QR) are visible throughout.

The ABC logo is visible in the bottom right corner throughout, and promotional overlays appear in the top left, including 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' and a QR code for 'Buy code'.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a black screen displaying the word 'THANKS' before transitioning to a grand awards ceremony stage, illuminated with golden lights and a sparkling circular backdrop. Three men in black tuxedos stand holding Oscar statuettes. The central figure, a man with a beard and dark hair (Man in Black Tuxedo with Bow Tie (Winner)), speaks into a microphone, expressing heartfelt gratitude to their producers, his beautiful wife Kate for standing by him, and the team of 600 artists at MPC and the fantastic special effects team, emphasizing that they share this award with them. He concludes with a sincere "Thank you." Another voice briefly adds, "Thank you all, thank you so much... thank you to the team, thank you." The camera cuts to the audience, showing a woman in a black off-the-shoulder gown with an updo, smiling warmly, and later, a woman with short gray hair and large hoop earrings listening intently beside a young man in a tuxedo. The scene then shifts to a different part of the stage where a woman in a shimmering light-pink one-shoulder gown (Woman in Pink Gown) and a man in a black tuxedo stand at a microphone. The woman speaks passionately, greeting the audience with "Good evening" and announcing they are there to present the award for Makeup and Hairstyling, highlighting the impossible task of overstating the role hair and makeup play in filmmaking. She then humorously adds, "Case in point? I'm 87 years old," followed by audience laughter, before introducing herself with "And I'm Charlize Theron." Throughout the segment, the ABC logo is visible in the bottom right corner, and the celebratory atmosphere is underscored by audience applause and reactions, including a woman in a black dress clapping and smiling, her blonde hair in a bun. The stage backdrop features dynamic, wavy silver patterns with warm lighting.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, identified as a host, speaking at a podium. In a clear, confident, and slightly humorous voice, he recounts, "Listen, all joking aside, I do want to say, I saw firsthand how talented hair and makeup people are in The Irishman. They would transform us every day. I would just sit there and be amazed." He then adds, "And then Pachi would come in and say..." before trailing off. A quick transition shows a woman in a shimmering light-pink one-shoulder gown standing beside him, also a host, listening attentively. She playfully interjects, "I, I think they're gonna believe that. Not everything is Netflix, right?" to which the man responds, "Sorry. Yeah. I got carried away." She then adds, "Everyone woke up there." As light orchestral music begins to swell, the woman formally announces, "Here are the wonderfully talented artists nominated this year for achievement in makeup and hairstyling." The camera cuts to a wide shot of the grand stage, revealing a large, curved screen above them displaying 'MAKEUP AND HAIRSTYLING' in illuminated letters, set against golden architectural elements. The scene transitions into a cinematic montage showcasing the nominees, with the woman's voice narrating each film title and its makeup and hairstyling team. segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens with a close-up of a swirling, abstract golden and silver backdrop, transitioning to a live awards ceremony stage. On stage, the Man in Black Tuxedo with Bow Tie (Host) and the Woman in Pink Gown stand as the host, with a clear male voice, announces the winner for 'MAKEUP AND HAIRSTYLING': "Bombshell! Kazu Hiro, Anne Morgan, and Vivian Baker." He adds that this is "the second Oscar and fourth nomination for Kazu Hiro," as enthusiastic applause and cheering erupt from the audience. The scene cuts to the audience, then back to the stage as the Man in Black Tuxedo with Bow Tie (Winner), identified as Kazu Hiro, walks confidently toward the podium, flanked by the Woman in Black Dress and the Woman in Blue Gown, both holding Oscar statuettes. At the podium, Kazu Hiro, holding his Oscar, delivers an earnest acceptance speech, his voice clear and appreciative. He begins by saying, "Thank you very much. It's a great honor," and proceeds to thank "members of Academy," director Jay Roach, Charles Randolph, Bob Graf, Karen Gocho, Beth Cono, AJ Ticks, Michelle Glack, Dave Johnson, the entire team at Fox Studio and Lionsgate, the casting crew, friends, family, and his mentors Rick Baker and Dick Smith. He then expresses "heartfelt thanks to Charisse Theron," calling her "amazing" and an "amazing actor and producer," crediting her "compassion and love and care" for making the film possible. He concludes by stating that "because of your bravery and passion, we are able to set the new bar in the makeup industry and create..." before the audio fades. Throughout his speech, the Woman in Black Dress, wearing a black sleeveless gown with a plunging neckline and glasses, and the Woman in Blue Gown, in a strapless royal blue gown with dangling earrings, stand beside him, listening attentively and holding their own Oscar statuettes. The grand stage backdrop features a golden, circular design with sparkling lights, and on-screen text confirms the award category and winners. The audience, including the Woman in Black Off-the-Shoulder Gown and the Woman with Short Gray Hair and Hoop Earrings, is seen clapping and smiling, their applause and cheers audible. Promotional overlays like 'Buy code' with a QR code, 'WATCH', and 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' appear in the top left corner, alongside the ABC logo in the bottom right, while the celebratory and formal atmosphere is enhanced by warm stage lighting.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, appearing emotional as he delivers an acceptance speech, expressing gratitude and stating, "we wouldn't be getting this award without you." The scene quickly transitions to a wide shot of a grand awards stage with a circular, illuminated backdrop featuring intricate, swirling patterns, where three winners—two women and one man—stand holding Oscar statuettes amidst the enthusiastic clapping and cheering of the formally dressed audience. A close-up shows a woman in a black off-the-shoulder gown smiling and clapping enthusiastically, before the winners walk off stage. Dramatic, upbeat music swells as an announcer's voice highlights upcoming performances, including Penelope Cruz and Elton John performing "Rocketman's 'I'm gonna love me again'," and later, the presentation of Oscars for Directing, Best Actor, Actress, and Best Picture. The golden, circular 'OSCARS' structure is illuminated as the announcer concludes, "The Oscars on ABC, brought to you by Cadillac." A woman in a black gown with a large white bow at the neckline then appears on stage, holding a card and speaking with a joyful expression, greeting the audience with "Good evening" and announcing the nominees for Best International Feature Film. This leads into a cinematic montage of the nominees, accompanied by the woman's voice: a close-up of a man’s intense blue eyes from 'Corpus Christi' (Poland), directed by Jan Komasa; a woman in a yellow top tending to beehives in 'Honeyland' (North Macedonia), directed by Ljubo Stefanov and Tamara Kotevska; and finally, a group of people celebrating in front of the Eiffel Tower in 'Les Misérables' (France), directed by Ladj Ly. Throughout the celebratory and grand atmosphere, with dynamic lighting, the ABC logo is visible in the bottom right corner, and on-screen text identifies each film’s title, country of origin, and director.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a cinematic montage, accompanied by a female voice announcing clips from international feature film nominees: 'Pain and Glory,' directed by Pedro Almodóvar, and 'Parasite' from South Korea, directed by Bong Joon Ho, with on-screen text identifying each. The scene transitions to the Oscars stage, where a large, illuminated 'INTERNATIONAL FEATURE FILM' sign glows against a grand, circular, starry backdrop. The Woman in Black Gown with White Bow, dressed in a black gown with a prominent white bow, stands at the podium, her clear voice declaring, "And the Oscar goes to... Parasite!" as the audience, including Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt, erupts in enthusiastic applause and cheers. She continues, noting, "This is the first movie from South Korea to be nominated and to win the Oscar." Bong Joon Ho, the director of 'Parasite,' is shown celebrating with Han Jin Won, his co-winner, in the audience before they rise and make their way to the stage. Bong Joon Ho, wearing a dark suit and glasses, accepts the golden Oscar statuette with a joyful expression, joined on stage by Han Jin Won and a Woman in Black Outfit, who also holds an Oscar. Bong Joon Ho delivers an emotional acceptance speech, first expressing gratitude in English with a Korean accent, saying, "Thank you. Ah, great honor," then speaking in Korean, which is subsequently interpreted. He raises the statuette triumphantly as the audience continues to cheer, with celebratory orchestral music playing throughout. The stage backdrop features a swirling, illuminated design, and the ABC logo remains visible in the bottom right corner, emphasizing the grandeur of the moment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Bong Joon Ho, a man in a black suit and glasses, holding an Oscar statuette, speaking into a microphone on stage. Beside him stands Han Jin Won, also in a dark suit, holding an Oscar, listening attentively, smiling, and nodding. The stage's dramatic backdrop features a swirling blue design with glowing golden lights. Bong Joon Ho's Korean speech is translated by a female voice, expressing his happiness as the first recipient of the "International Feature Film" award under its new name and acknowledging the new direction this change symbolizes. He then thanks the "wonderful actors and staff" who made the film, specifically naming beloved cast members like Song Kang-ho, Lee Sun-kyun, Choi Woo-shik, Jang Hye-jin, Park So-dam, and Lee Jung-eun. As the translator relays his gratitude to the "loving crew members and cast members," the camera cuts to a wide shot of the audience, showing enthusiastic clapping and cheers from notable figures such as Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt, all in formal attire and smiling warmly. The loud applause and cheers continue as the camera returns to Bong Joon Ho, who continues his speech, thanking cinematographer Hong Kyung-pyo, production designer Lee Ha-jun, editor Yang Jin-mo, and all the artists, a sentiment echoed by the translator. He concludes by thanking Barunson, CJ, and Neon for helping realize their vision. Han Jin Won claps in support throughout. An ABC logo is visible in the bottom right, while promotional overlays like 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' and 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE' appear in the top left, indicating a fan-edited compilation. The overall atmosphere is one of triumph and global celebration for 'Parasite.'

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Bong Joon Ho, wearing a black suit and glasses, beaming with emotion as he holds an Oscar statuette on a grand stage with a circular, illuminated backdrop. He thanks "everyone at CJ and who allowed me to actualize my vision," followed by loud applause and cheering from the packed audience. As the camera pulls back to show the full stage, he adds, "I'm ready, ready to drink tonight," eliciting laughter and more applause before he concludes, "Until next morning. Thank you." The audience, including a smiling woman in a black off-the-shoulder gown and a clapping woman in a gold sequined gown, continues to cheer enthusiastically as Bong Joon Ho raises his Oscar in triumph. The scene transitions to a different award presentation, where three women stand on stage: one in a shimmering silver gown, one in a green off-the-shoulder satin gown, and one in a black lace top with a pink skirt. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dark, close-up shot of a man's face, transitioning to a dimly lit room where a man in a white shirt and dark jacket stands near a glowing blue screen, displaying the on-screen text 'JOKER' by Hildur Guðnadóttir, accompanied by orchestral music with an eerie string sound. The scene shifts to a grand awards ceremony in a large, ornate theater filled with a formally dressed audience, where a woman, Hildur Guðnadóttir, in a black gown with a sparkling silver sequined bodice, walks confidently to the stage, holding an Oscar statuette. As she begins her emotional acceptance speech, the audience, including notable figures like Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt, applauds warmly and cheers loudly. Promotional text overlays like 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' are visible in the top left corner. Close-ups capture Hildur’s emotional expression as she speaks, with the ABC logo visible in the bottom right corner. Her voice, filled with gratitude, states, "This is so touching. Thank you, the Academy for welcoming me so warmly. It's just, I don't even, I don't know what to say. My fellow nominees, masters of the craft, it's been such an honor to get to know you all. You're so special. A film composer is only as creative as the dialogue with their director. Todd Phillips, we're so..." The stage backdrop is a warm gradient of red and gold, and on-screen text identifies the award as 'ORIGINAL SCORE' for 'JOKER' by Hildur Guðnadóttir. The audience continues to cheer and applaud, and Hildur smiles gratefully, holding the statuette close. The segment concludes with a wide shot of the audience standing and applauding, emphasizing the celebratory atmosphere of the event.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Hildur Guðnadóttir, radiant in a black gown with a heavily embellished silver sequined bodice, her short dark hair in an updo, as she stands at a grand, gold-accented podium, holding her Oscar statuette. Her voice, filled with emotion and gratitude, begins her acceptance speech for the Original Score award for 'Joker'. She thanks her family, her 'incredible husband Sam,' her 'love, my best friend, my other set of ears,' and her mother and son Koden, expressing profound love with a joyful laugh. Throughout her speech, the audience, including notable figures like Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt, are seen smiling warmly and applauding enthusiastically. The stage is bathed in a warm red and gold gradient, with the ABC logo visible in the bottom right corner. On-screen promotional text overlays, such as 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' and 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE,' appear in the top left. Hildur then delivers a powerful message to 'the girls, to the women, to the mothers, to the daughters who hear the music bubbling within,' urging them to 'please speak up, we need to hear your voices,' which is met with sustained, roaring applause and cheers. The segment concludes with a wide shot of the stage, the large 'ORIGINAL SONG' sign illuminated above (despite the award being for Original Score), as the audience continues their joyous applause, celebrating the prestigious moment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dark, sweeping shot of a grand, circular stage illuminated by golden light streaks and a starry backdrop, transitioning to a wide view of the audience in a dimly lit auditorium, filled with the sound of applause, cheering, and whooping. The camera then focuses on the Man in Black Tuxedo with Bow Tie (Host), now dressed in a distinctive purple suit with yellow trim, a black bow tie, a black hat, and purple-rimmed glasses, standing at a microphone on the stage. He speaks animatedly, gesturing with his right hand, saying, "Last time I was on this stage, I had the honor of jumping to arms by Morehouse brother, Mr. Samuel Jackson," followed by laughter and applause as he adds, "And thank God he didn't drop me." With a glowing blue Oscar statuette as a backdrop, he continues, "Stand up here tonight for hearing the nominees for achievement in directing," as applause and building orchestral music accompany his words. The scene cuts to a montage of behind-the-scenes footage from various films, introduced by the host's voice and accompanied by the continuing music and applause: Martin Scorsese for 'The Irishman', shown directing a scene; Todd Phillips for 'Joker', in a casual meeting; Sam Mendes for '1917', directing on a war-torn set; and Quentin Tarantino for 'Once Upon a Time... in Hollywood', directing on a sunny street. The montage concludes with Bong Joon Ho for 'Parasite', shown in a kitchen scene with a family. The segment culminates with a return to the stage, where the word 'DIRECTING' appears in large, illuminated letters above the host, who then presents the nominees for Best Director, repeating "Bong Joon Ho!" to particularly loud applause and cheering. A graphic displays the five nominees: Martin Scorsese, Todd Phillips, Sam Mendes, Quentin Tarantino, and Bong Joon Ho, each with their film title and a still image from the audience, while the ABC logo remains visible in the bottom right corner throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a nominee display screen showing 'MARTIN SCORSESE' and 'TODD PHILLIPS' for the Directing category, as enthusiastic applause and cheering erupt. A female announcer's voice declares, "This is the second Oscar tonight and the third nomination for Bong Joon Ho." The camera then cuts to the audience, where Bong Joon Ho, a man in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, is seen rising from his seat, surrounded by clapping attendees including a woman in a green off-the-shoulder satin gown and a woman in a black lace top with a pink skirt. He makes his way through the crowd, smiling and waving, to the continued sound of fervent applause and cheering. A behind-the-scenes clip of Bong Joon Ho in a kitchen scene from 'Parasite' is shown with on-screen text, before he ascends the stage. There, he is greeted by a woman in a black outfit and a man in a dark suit (Han Jin Won), both smiling, against a deep blue, starry backdrop with swirling light patterns and a prominent 'DIRECTING' sign. Bong Joon Ho, now at the podium wearing a black suit and glasses, is visibly emotional, wiping his eyes and covering his face with his hand. He speaks, first saying "Thank you" in English, then continuing in Korean. A female translator's voice conveys his sentiment: "After winning Best International Feature, I thought I was done for the day and was ready to relax." The audience, including Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt, is shown clapping and smiling warmly, as Bong Joon Ho continues speaking in Korean. Wide shots emphasize the grand, celebratory atmosphere of the tiered auditorium, with the ABC logo remaining in the bottom right corner.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Bong Joon Ho, visibly emotional in a black tuxedo and glasses, wiping his eyes and covering his face at the podium on the Oscars stage, which features a dark, starry backdrop illuminated by blue and golden light streaks. The ABC logo is visible in the bottom right, while intermittent on-screen text overlays like 'Buy code' with a QR, 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO,' and 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE' appear. Bong Joon Ho begins to speak in Korean, translated by an off-screen voice, expressing gratitude and recalling a saying he "carved deep into [his] heart: the most personal is the most creative." As he continues, the camera cuts to a wide shot of the formally dressed audience, where Tom Hanks claps warmly, followed by Al Pacino, also smiling and clapping. The atmosphere is celebratory, with many attendees standing and applauding enthusiastically. Bong Joon Ho then reveals, through his translator, that the quote is from "our great Martin Scorsese," which elicits a massive wave of cheers, whistles, and sustained applause from the crowd, further emphasizing the celebratory mood. The camera pans across the excited audience before returning to Bong Joon Ho, who, still emotional, continues in Korean, translated as him being "terrified watching Martin's films in school" and never expecting to win, considering it an honor just to be nominated alongside Scorsese. He gestures occasionally, standing on the circular stage with glowing white edges, as the lighting shifts between warm reds and cool blues. The sequence concludes with a close-up of Bong Joon Ho, still visibly moved, as the audience's fervent cheering persists.

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens with a close-up of Bong Joon Ho, visibly emotional, wiping his face with his hand as he stands on a grand awards ceremony stage with a dark, starry backdrop and blue lighting accents. Beside him, a woman in a black outfit, likely a co-winner or collaborator, stands smiling. The woman translates, stating it was 'a huge honor' and that she 'never thought I would win,' as Bong Joon Ho, wearing a black tuxedo and glasses, begins his acceptance speech in Korean. The camera cuts to the audience, showing Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt, all smiling warmly and clapping enthusiastically. The focus returns to Bong Joon Ho, who continues his speech in Korean, which the woman translates, mentioning how 'Quentin always put my films on his list' when people in the US were unfamiliar with them, adding 'He's here. Thank you so much.' Bong Joon Ho then directly addresses 'Quentin' in English, saying 'I love you,' which elicits laughter and enthusiastic applause from the audience. He gestures with his hands, holding an Oscar statuette, and continues his speech in Korean. The translator conveys his humorous desire to get a 'Texas chainsaw' to 'split the Oscar trophy into five' and share it with 'Todd and Sam,' whom he admires, if the Academy allows. This prompts more laughter and applause from the audience, including Al Pacino and Tom Hanks, who react with visible delight. The atmosphere is celebratory and emotional, with the ABC logo consistently displayed in the bottom right corner.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Bong Joon Ho, visibly emotional, wiping his face as he stands on stage, holding an Oscar statuette, and speaking with an accent, humorously stating, "Thank you. I will drink until next morning. Thank you," to loud applause and cheering. The camera quickly cuts to the audience, where Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt are seen clapping and smiling warmly. The scene transitions to Bong Joon Ho on stage, receiving the award from a presenter in a purple suit with yellow trim and a black hat. Amidst sustained applause and swelling orchestral music, a formal female voice announces, "Please welcome three-time Academy Award winner and recipient of the Academy's Governors Award, Steven Spielberg." The camera pans across the grand, circular stage with a starry, blue-lit backdrop, showing the vast audience in formal attire, clapping enthusiastically. The host, now in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, walks across the stage, holding a microphone, and addresses the crowd. A male voice with a British accent, identifying himself as George MacKay, speaks from the balcony, greeting the audience, "Evening. As we celebrate the artistic highlights of the past year... Welcome back. I'm George MacKay and I'm hanging up here in the balcony. Is everyone having a good time?" to further cheers. He continues, "Well, we're coming to the final three awards of the evening and time is of the essence, which is why I'm here to introduce myself, before introducing someone else, who will in turn introduce someone else. The winner, Olivia Colman," followed by loud applause and orchestral music. Wide-angle shots capture the ornate, golden, circular stage design with sparkling lights and tiered seating, while on-screen text overlays 'TV 14 DLV', 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO', and 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE' appear in the top left corner, all contributing to the grand and celebratory atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens on a black screen displaying a promotional overlay 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE' in red and white text, as a whimsical, upbeat instrumental tune with a prominent flute melody and percussion begins to play. The scene then transitions to a grand awards stage, bathed in warm ambient lighting, featuring a circular golden design and sparkling vertical light strands. A woman with short, platinum blonde hair, wearing a dark navy velvet gown with cut-out shoulders and circular brooches, walks confidently to a microphone at the stage's center, holding a white card. She smiles warmly and begins to speak in a clear, engaging British accent, humorously stating, "Winning an Oscar ages you," which elicits laughter and applause from the unseen audience. As she continues, "Hello guys. Um, thank you for having me back. It's such a pleasure to be here. Last year was the best night of my husband's life," the camera alternates between wide shots of the stage and close-ups of her expressive face and joyful demeanor, capturing her gestures. Throughout her speech, various on-screen text overlays appear in the top left corner, including 'Buy code' with a QR code, 'TV 14 DLV', and 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO', while the ABC logo is visible in the bottom right. She further quips, "He actually says that. And I've given birth three times," drawing more laughter and applause. The woman maintains her enthusiastic presentation, occasionally glancing at her card, concluding, "So I, I hope you're all having as good a time as he did. And I did. But um, I don't want to keep you from the bar much longer. I know I'm supposed to talk about acting and find some clever thread connecting these performances, but their beauty and brilliance surely lies in the fact that there isn't one. And I'm sorry," as the grand stage backdrop with its large, curved golden structure and glowing lights enhances the celebratory atmosphere, and the whimsical music continues to play.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dark, blurred shot, quickly transitioning to a cinematic montage accompanied by an upbeat, slightly dramatic background score. A man in a dark suit with a serious expression is seen in a dimly lit environment, followed by a close-up of a hand holding a cigarette, as a male voice asks, "You didn't ask the same questions every week. That's your job." The scene shifts to a man with graying hair and a beard, wearing a white shirt, speaking earnestly in a warmly lit room, with the on-screen text 'but really I don't hold a grudge,' as he is heard saying in Spanish, "Todos querían matarlo, pero realmente no le guardo rencor" (Everyone wanted to kill him, but really I don't hold a grudge). Across from him, a man with long dark hair and a beard in a black sweater listens intently. A female voice then states, "This is the name. He needs to know that I fought for him." The setting changes to a bright, sunlit outdoor scene where a man in a denim jacket stands beside a 'Musso & Frank Grill' sign, marked 'BEST IN HOLLYWOOD.' The montage continues with a man in a light-colored shirt and sunglasses looking into the distance, followed by a man in a dark suit with a red tie, speaking passionately, heard declaring, "This is the face of failure that is your crying about... Jesus built walls. His face is the face of mercy. Mercy is the dynamite that blows down walls." A calm male voice then reflects on someone "coming to terms with what it's like to be slightly more..." The scene cuts to a man in a white robe shaking hands with another in a black robe with red trim in a grand, ornate hall. The segment then shows a man in a dark shirt in a dimly lit room, followed by a man with graying hair and a beard in a green shirt with red trim, speaking seriously, with the on-screen text 'Control the emotion. Don't cry,' as he is heard advising in Spanish, "Controla la emoción. No llores. No es mejor actor el que llora, sino el que lucha por contener las lágrimas" (Control the emotion. Don't cry. The best actor is not the one who cries, but the one who fights to hold back tears). An agitated male voice then says, "I don't know why everyone is so rude... I don't want anything from you. Maybe a little bit of warmth, maybe a hug..." The segment concludes with a close-up of a man with long dark hair in a black shirt, looking distressed and holding his head in his hands, as the background music swells and ends. The ABC logo remains visible in the bottom right corner throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a man's intense, dark eyes, followed by a dramatic scene where a man with gray hair, wearing a red sweater, yells aggressively at a woman with short blonde hair in a dimly lit room, accompanied by a male voice shouting, 'Every day I wake up, I help your debt. If I can guarantee Henry would be okay, I don't give a damn if I get an illness, if I can get by that door and die.' The scene then shifts to a close-up of another man's face, before cutting to a cardinal in a grand, ornate church, bowing his head solemnly, as a calm, melancholic male voice states, 'Make our ground to peace. I remind you, the truth may be vital, but without love, it is unbearable.' Dramatic orchestral music swells as the setting changes to a dazzling awards ceremony stage with a circular, starry backdrop and golden light streaks. A large screen displays a montage of film characters, including a clown-faced man and the man in the red sweater, while applause begins to build. A female announcer then declares, 'Here are the nominees for performance by an actor in a leading role.' The camera pans to the audience, showing Antonio Banderas in a black tuxedo, looking thoughtful, as the announcer names him for 'Pain and Glory,' followed by warm applause and cheering. Leonardo DiCaprio is then shown clapping warmly as he is announced for 'Once Upon a Time... in Hollywood,' also met with applause. Adam Driver appears with a serious expression as he is nominated for 'Marriage Story,' and Jonathan Pryce, with white hair and glasses, claps with a smile as he is named for 'The Two Popes.' The segment concludes with a split-screen graphic listing all the nominees for 'Actor in a Leading Role' (Antonio Banderas, Leonardo DiCaprio, Adam Driver, Joaquin Phoenix, and Jonathan Pryce), as the announcer dramatically states, 'And the Oscar goes to... Joaquin Phoenix, 'Joker',' prompting loud applause and cheering. The ABC logo and promotional text ('Buy code' with a QR code, 'TV 14 DLV', and 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO') are visible throughout the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a black screen displaying a 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' prompt, then transitions to a vibrant awards ceremony. A female announcer's voice declares, "The first Oscar and four nominations for Joaquin Phoenix," as Joaquin Phoenix, dressed in a classic black tuxedo with a bow tie, walks onto the warmly lit stage amidst a standing ovation, enthusiastic clapping, and celebratory music from the formally dressed audience. He approaches the podium, visibly emotional, covering his mouth and adjusting his hair. On-screen text overlays like 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE', a 'Buy code' QR code, and 'TV 14 DLV' are visible. The camera cuts to the audience, showing Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt clapping warmly. Phoenix begins his acceptance speech for 'Actor in a Leading Role' for 'Joker', his voice slightly raspy and emotional, stating he feels "so much better at this right now" and expressing humility, not feeling "elevated above any of my fellow nominees or anyone in this room." He shares a love for film, acknowledging that this "form of expression has given me the most extraordinary life," and emphasizes the "greatest gift" is the "opportunity to use our voice for the voiceless," reflecting on "distressing issues that we are facing collectively." The grand circular stage backdrop sparkles with golden lights, and the ABC logo is visible in the bottom right corner, underscoring the celebratory and intimate atmosphere.

Dressed in a classic black tuxedo with a bow tie, his graying, wavy hair and beard frame an intense, contemplative expression. He begins by stating, "we feel or are made to feel that we champion different causes. But for me, I see commonality." As the camera pulls back, he stands at a podium on a grand stage, illuminated by warm, golden bokeh lights, frequently touching his face and adjusting his hair. He passionately declares, "whether we're talking about gender inequality or racism or queer rights or indigenous rights or animal rights, we're talking about the fight against injustice." He elaborates on this, fighting "the belief that one nation, one people, one race, one gender, or one species has the right to dominate, control, and use and exploit another with impunity." The ABC logo is visible in the bottom right corner. The camera alternates between tight close-ups of his eyes and mouth, capturing his emotional delivery, and wider shots of the vast, formally dressed audience, including attentive figures like Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt. A powerful, sustained applause erupts from the audience, their hands raised in appreciation, creating a powerful sense of shared recognition. As the applause subsides, Phoenix continues, stating, "we've become very disconnected from the natural world and many of us, what we're guilty of is an egocentric worldview. The belief that we're the center of the universe. We go into the natural world and we plunder it for its resources." He concludes the segment by saying, "We feel..."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a dark, blurred shot that quickly transitions to a wide view of a grand awards ceremony stage, where a formally dressed audience, including notable figures like Tom Hanks, Al Pacino, and Brad Pitt, is seated in tiered rows, clapping warmly to the faint orchestral music. Joaquin Phoenix, a man with graying, wavy hair and a beard, wearing a black tuxedo with a bow tie, walks onto the stage and stands at a microphone, his expression solemn and reflective. He begins his acceptance speech for the Actor in a Leading Role award for 'Joker', speaking with intense emotion about animal cruelty, stating, "we artificially inseminate a cow, and when she gives birth, we steal her baby... we take her milk that's intended for her calf, and we put it in our coffee and our cereal." He then transitions to human nature, asserting, "we fear the idea of personal change because we think that we have to sacrifice something to give something up," but emphasizes that "human beings at our best are so inventive and creative and ingenious." As he continues, the camera alternates between close-ups of his face, capturing his intense gaze and emotional delivery, and wider shots showing him on the stage with a backdrop of golden, swirling lights and a large, circular, illuminated design. He gestures with his hands, occasionally touching his face or adjusting his hair, as he advocates for using "love and compassion as our guiding principles" to "create, develop and implement systems of change that are beneficial to all sentient beings and to the environment." He concludes this segment with a personal reflection, admitting, "I've been selfish, I've been cruel at times, hard to work with," but expresses gratitude that "so many of you in this room have given me a second chance," believing "that's when we're at our best when we support..." On-screen text overlays, including a 'Buy code' QR code and 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE', appear intermittently, while the ABC logo remains visible in the bottom right corner, all contributing to an atmosphere of prestige and emotional weight.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a black screen before transitioning to a grand awards ceremony stage, where Renée Zellweger, identified by a lower-third graphic as the winner of 'Actress in a Leading Role' for 'Judy', stands at a microphone holding an Oscar statuette. Wearing a shimmering light gray one-shoulder gown, she is bathed in warm, golden bokeh lights against a dark backdrop, creating a celebratory and elegant atmosphere. With a voice filled with emotion, gratitude, and a hint of tears, Renée thanks "the Academy" and expresses her appreciation for being invited to such a "special" and "meaningful" experience. The camera alternates between close-ups of her tearful eyes and smiling lips, and wider shots revealing the grand stage with its circular golden design and tiered audience, who are seen clapping and watching attentively. She acknowledges her fellow nominees, "Cynthia, Scarlett, Charlize, Saoirse," stating it's "an honor to be considered in your company." The ABC logo is visible in the bottom right corner, while promotional text like 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' and a QR code appear in the top left. Renée continues, thanking "David Livingston, Cameron McCracken, Director Rupert Goold," expressing pride in working on the "beautiful movie." She then extends her gratitude to "my stage moms, Matt, Paul, Gary, Jessie Buckley, Finn Wittrock, Rufus Sewell," and "all cast and the crew who worked so hard on this film to celebrate Judy Garland," before concluding with thanks to her "extended family at CAA, Peter Bryan and Kevin." The overall mood is one of heartfelt triumph and celebration.

segments.integrated_caption: A visibly emotional Renée Zellweger, her eyes glistening with tears, stands at a podium in a shimmering light gray one-shoulder gown, her blonde hair in an elegant updo, holding an Oscar statuette. As she speaks into the microphone, her expression shifts between joy, gratitude, and emotion, occasionally wiping her eyes. Her voice, filled with heartfelt emotion, thanks "Nancy Ryder" for 25 years and "John Carabino," her "handsome date of 25 years," for always "dreaming bigger" and bringing it back to "work, joy, and gratitude." She acknowledges her "immigrant folks who came here with nothing but each other and a belief in the American Dream," and extends gratitude to "Big Brother Drew, Randy, Judy, Stone, and Ava" for "all the love and support possible." She reflects that the past year of conversations celebrating Judy Garland "across genders, generations, and cultures" has been a "really cool reminder that our heroes unite us." The background features dark, golden bokeh lights and a grand, circular stage backdrop with golden light streaks, enhancing the celebratory mood. Her voice is heard, expressing gratitude to Nancy Ryder, John Carabino (her date), her immigrant family, and others like Big Brother Drew, Randy, Judy, Stone, and Ava, while reflecting on the unifying power of heroes through the celebration of Judy Garland.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens from a black screen, transitioning to a close-up of Renée Zellweger, who stands emotionally at a podium on a grand stage, holding her Oscar statuette. She is resplendent in a shimmering light gray one-shoulder gown, her blonde hair styled in an elegant updo, as the stage glows with warm, golden lights. A large, curved backdrop prominently displays 'BEST ACTRESS' in bold, glowing letters, while a softly lit image of her face adds a personal touch behind her. With tearful eyes and subtle gestures, Renée delivers her acceptance speech, her voice filled with emotion as she speaks of those who “inspire us to find the best in ourselves.” The camera alternates between intimate close-ups of her expressive face and wider shots revealing the vast, darkened, tiered audience, all captivated by her words. She continues, listing a diverse array of heroes like “Neil Armstrong, Sally Ride, Dolores Huerta, Venus and Serena, and Selena, Bob Dylan, Scorsese, Fred Rogers, Harriet Tubman,” emphasizing how “we agree on our teachers” and “our courageous men and women in uniform who serve,” including “first responders and firefighters.” She explains that “when we celebrate our heroes, we are reminded of who we are as one people, united.” The ABC logo remains visible in the bottom right corner throughout. Renée concludes by noting that “Judy Garland did not receive this honor in her time,” but she is “certain that this moment is an extension of the celebration of her legacy.” The atmosphere is both celebratory and intimate, with the spotlight effect underscoring the significance of her presence.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of Renée Zellweger, wearing a shimmering light gray one-shoulder gown, standing at a podium on a grand stage with a dark, starry backdrop accented by golden bokeh lights. She holds an Oscar statuette and delivers an emotional acceptance speech, her voice tearful and reflective, frequently gesturing with her free hand as she speaks of "her legacy," "unique exceptionalism," "inclusivity," and "generosity of spirit," addressing "Miss Garland" as one of the "heroes who unite and define us," before thanking everyone. The camera alternates between tight close-ups of her face and medium shots showing her at the microphone, while the ABC logo is visible in the bottom right corner. Loud, celebratory applause and music swell as the scene then cuts to the audience, where Leonardo DiCaprio, in a black tuxedo with a bow tie, is seen clapping warmly with a slight smile. Renée Zellweger is then seen walking off stage with a man in a black tuxedo, likely a presenter, as they exit the stage area. The video transitions to a wide shot of the stage, revealing a large, curved screen above the podium displaying the words 'BEST PICTURE' in bold, glowing letters, set against a backdrop of golden, swirling architectural elements and tiered seating. An announcer's voice declares, "Coming up next, the final award of the evening, the Academy Award for Best Picture. It's the Oscars on ABC," as the audience is shown in a wide-angle view, clapping enthusiastically to the continued celebratory music. On-screen text overlays appear intermittently, including 'Buy code' with a QR code, 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO', and 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE', indicating the video is part of a fan-edited or promotional compilation. The atmosphere is celebratory and prestigious, with warm lighting and a sense of grandeur, underscored by the ongoing applause and orchestral music.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a smiling man in the audience, followed by a transition to a grand, celebratory stage adorned with golden arches and twinkling lights, featuring a large, illuminated Oscar statuette. A Woman in Red Ruffled Gown, with short platinum blonde hair and red gloves, stands at a podium, speaking with a composed and warm expression. Her clear voice is heard thanking the audience, stating, "Thank you very much," and adding, "Nothing is more important than raising awareness, right?" amidst applause. She continues, explaining, "Tonight, we've hopefully brought to light the impact that films have made and can make on our lives as individuals and on society as a whole." She then announces, "For the Academy, here are the films that made the greatest impact this year," prompting more applause. The scene cuts to a high-octane cinematic montage from 'Ford v Ferrari,' showcasing vintage cars like a white and orange Ford GT40 and a black Ferrari racing intensely on a track under a bright sky. Dynamic camera angles capture close-ups of a helmeted driver and intense expressions, accompanied by dramatic orchestral music and dialogue from the film, including a male voice exclaiming, "You ready? Get it. Incredible," and later stating, "There are two people who find something they have to do. Tell them they can't do it, it's gonna drive them clean out of their mind." Another male voice adds, "Can't make him give up," and "Not a good job." On-screen text identifies the film as 'FORD v FERRARI' and lists its producers: Peter Chernin, Jenno Topping, and James Mangold. The montage concludes with a dramatic shot of a car speeding down the track, after which the Woman in Red Ruffled Gown returns to announce, "Ford versus Ferrari. Peter Chernin, Jenno Topping, and James Mangold, producers," to further applause. Throughout the segment, the ABC logo is visible in the bottom right corner, and promotional text overlays like 'Buy code' with a QR code and 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO' appear in the top left.

segments.integrated_caption: Upbeat, slightly tense orchestral music begins as the segment opens with a close-up of a man in a dark suit, his face partially obscured, creating a mysterious atmosphere while male voices declare, 'We're going to war with these people,' and 'But nobody's like this offer.' The scene quickly transitions to a nighttime cityscape featuring an illuminated building and a parking lot of yellow taxis, followed by a chaotic crowd inside a dimly lit hall, where people are holding signs and shouting. A female voice then announces, 'The Irishman,' as a male voice lists 'Martin Scorsese, Robert De Niro, Jane Rosenthal, and Emma Tillinger Koskoff, producers,' coinciding with a man in a suit holding a phone to his ear with a serious expression, indicating high stakes. The narrative shifts to a young boy in a Hitler Youth uniform, looking confused or fearful, interacting with a woman in a patterned coat in a Nazi-era town square, as female voices assert, 'He's not a Nazi,' and 'I'm surrounded by swastikas, so I think that's a pretty good sign right there.' A male voice shouts, 'Do not let your German mind be bossed around!' as the boy appears distressed in a bedroom with the woman behind him, prompting a female voice to respond, 'I won't let her boss my German brain around,' and another to add, 'Definitely not a good time to be a Nazi.' A man in a suit with glasses is then seen seated at a table, writing, before a female voice announces, 'Jojo Rabbit,' and a male voice lists 'Carthew Neal, Taika Waititi, Chelsea Winstanley, producers.' The video then introduces a man with green hair and clown makeup, smiling widely, as male voices ask, 'Tell us a joke,' and 'What do you get... when you cross a mentally ill loner with a society that abandons him and treats him like trash?' A close-up of a woman with dark hair, her expression intense, is shown as a female voice shouts, 'I'll tell you what you get! You get what you deserve!' The final frames feature the clown-faced man in a red suit, surrounded by a cheering crowd, with on-screen text identifying the film as 'Joker' and listing producers Todd Phillips, Bradley Cooper, and Emma Tillinger Koskoff, while a female voice begins to speak about women having 'minds, and... souls, as well as just hearts. And they've got ambition and they've got...' The ABC logo is visible throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens with a dark, indistinct frame before transitioning to a period scene featuring a woman in a yellow dress with a ruffled collar in a warmly lit, ornate interior, as a passionate female voice declares, "Love is just beauty, and I'm so sick of people saying that love is just all a woman is for. I'm so sick of it." This is followed by an outdoor grassy field where a man in a vest and white shirt walks toward a woman in a brown coat, then a lively outdoor gathering with people in period attire celebrating around a cake. On-screen text identifies the film as 'LITTLE WOMEN' and an announcer states, "Little Women. Amy Pascal, producer." The narrative then cuts to a modern kitchen where a man in a white shirt holds a piece of paper, followed by a man in a dark suit standing in a minimalist room with a coffee maker. A distressed female voice is heard saying, "I don't want to be married," followed by a calm male voice reflecting, "I'll never get to really be his parents again. It'll be different. It won't exist." A woman in a blue top and beige cardigan appears, gesturing expressively in a bright, modern dining area, as an accusing female voice states, "You wouldn't love me as much as I love you," met by a defensive male voice asking, "What does that have to do with LA?" and an angry female voice asserting, "You're so merged with your own selfishness! You don't need to identify it as selfishness anymore!" An announcer then identifies this segment as "Marriage Story." The segment abruptly shifts to a war-torn battlefield, showing a soldier in a brown uniform crawling through mud and debris amidst explosions and gunfire, then running across a desolate field as other soldiers follow. On-screen text identifies the film as '1917' and an announcer states, "1917. Sam Mendes, Pippa Harris, Jayne-Ann Tenggren, and Callum McDougall, producers." A calm male voice reflects, "journey is over. I think we've reached the end of the trail cliff," followed by an intense female voice exclaiming, "You are the devil! And I'm here to do the devil's..." The final frame shows a man in a dark shirt sitting in a dimly lit room, possibly reflecting on the events, with a pitcher on a table beside him. Throughout the segment, the ABC logo is visible in the bottom right corner, and promotional text overlays appear in the top left, including 'Buy code' with a QR code, 'PLEASE LIKE & SHARE THIS VIDEO', and 'DON'T FORGET TO FACEBOOK CHUMO DAM SUBSCRIBE'.
